segments.title: A practical guide to medieval life
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke introduces Tony Mount's approachable guide to medieval English society, from housing and food to law, family life, and warfare.
segments.body: The book is organized around the practical questions a person would face in medieval England rather than around a conventional political narrative. Its chapters move through class, religion, clothing, health, work, family, war, and law, giving viewers a useful map of everyday conditions that shaped medieval communities. The warfare chapter is presented alongside the social institutions that supported or constrained it, rather than as an isolated account of fighting.

evidence.note: The review lists the book's practical chapters, including its section on warfare.

segments.title: Animals as household workers and companions
segments.summary: The pets chapter shows how livestock, horses, dogs, cats, and companion animals were woven into medieval household work and daily comfort.
segments.body: The review begins its chosen chapter by placing animals inside the medieval household economy. Cows, goats, and chickens could share domestic space, while horses and oxen supplied traction, dogs guarded property and herded sheep, and cats protected stored grain from vermin. Alongside those working roles, the book treats companionship as a real reason for keeping animals, including less familiar pets such as squirrels, birds, and monkeys.

evidence.note: The passage contrasts livestock and working animals with companion animals.

segments.title: Cats between pest control and suspicion
segments.summary: Cats appear as useful mousers and affordable fur, but also as animals burdened by moral suspicion and religious imagery.
segments.body: This part of the review draws out the contradictory place of cats in medieval life. They controlled rats and mice and their fur was available to people below the elite, yet a hunting treatise could cast the cat as devilish because it tormented prey. The examples also show practical acceptance: Exeter Cathedral paid cats a small weekly allowance and provided an opening for them to pursue vermin. A guide for enclosed women, purchases recorded by aristocrats, and manuscript illustrations reveal how ideas about cats varied by setting and status.

evidence.note: The review discusses cats' vermin-control role, fur, religious associations, and documentary examples.

segments.title: Dogs, work, loyalty, and medieval medicine
segments.summary: A discussion of dogs links practical tracking and guarding duties with stories of loyalty and striking medieval medical beliefs.
segments.body: Dogs are presented first as working animals that could track forest game, guard sheep, and defend an owner's property. The review then contrasts familiar stories of canine loyalty with the politically awkward tale of Richard II's dog Math, which transferred its allegiance to Henry IV after the king's deposition. It also preserves the book's reminder that medieval medical ideas could be harsh and alien to modern readers, including remedies that credited dogs or puppies with healing properties.

evidence.note: The review covers dogs' working duties, classical loyalty tales, Richard II's dog, and medical remedies.

segments.title: Lap dogs as status and memorial companions
segments.summary: The review uses lap dogs in tombs and memorial brasses to show how pets could signal status, comfort, and enduring affection.
segments.body: Small dogs served as companions and visible status symbols for elite women, as well as supplying warmth in cold, draughty buildings. The book's memorial examples turn those private relationships into public evidence: dogs appear at the feet of women in tomb sculpture and on brasses in Sussex and Derbyshire. Dr. Clarke notes that the custom was not confined to England, widening the point from household affection to a broader medieval aristocratic culture of commemoration.

evidence.note: The passage describes lap dogs, tombs and brasses, and similar memorial customs beyond England.

segments.title: Why the book earns a recommendation
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke recommends Tony Mount's lively medieval-history guide while noting one minor error in its copyright statement.
segments.body: The closing assessment is emphatic: the book is enjoyable, worthwhile, and strong enough to make the reviewer seek out more of Tony Mount's work. Dr. Clarke briefly outlines Mount's background in historical nonfiction, teaching, medieval medicine, costume interpretation, and historical fiction. The only flaw he identifies is a small gender error in the copyright declaration, not a problem with the historical material discussed in the review.

evidence.note: The review evaluates the book, summarizes the author's background, and identifies the minor copyright-statement error.
